title: Trevor Bissert
text:
I am a PhD student in the Developmental and Brain Sciences Program at the University of Massachusetts, Boston, working in the Early Minds Lab with Dr. Zsuzsa Kaldy and Dr. Erik Blaser. My research focuses on how children strategically balance working memory and external resources using naturalistic paradigms and eye-tracking.
Previously, I worked in the Labs of Cognitive Neuroscience at Boston Children’s Hospital on projects investigating the development of executive functioning in US and Bangladeshi children using EEG, fNIRS, and eye-tracking. I’m interested in the development of early cognitive mechanisms behind memory and attention and how they interact with the environment. My passion lies in methodology, using eye-tracking and neuroimaging for a multimodal perspective on cognition.
In my free time I love trying new foods with my partner and traveling as much as we can. I’m also a plant parent to over 50 plant babies!

title: Projects
text:
This project investigates whether individual differences in how children (EM9_K) and adults (EM9_A) balance internal working memory and using external resources — the sampling–remembering trade-off — are stable across time and context. In naturalistic tasks, participants can choose to remember information or sample from an external source; we ask whether the strategies people adopt in that trade-off persist across similar tasks and short periods of time.





An adapted version of the Shopping Game paradigm (Koolhaas et al., 2026; Liang et al., 2025; Blaser & Kaldy, 2024). Participants use a touchscreen to shop in a virtual store using a shopping list that they can toggle to and from. We measure how much each participant chooses to remember versus revisit the list and whether these strategies are stable across time






A second naturalistic paradigm paired with the Shopping Game to probe near-transfer stability. Participants complete analogous memory-use decisions in a zoo-escape scenario, allowing us to test whether individual memory strategies persist beyond a single task.

title: Projects
text:
EM8 Pilots
This project investigated the same core question as EM9: whether individual differences in how people balance internal working memory and using external resources — the sampling–remembering trade-off — are stable across time and context. EM8 comprised pilot studies with adults (EM8_1) and children (EM8_2) using adapted versions of the Shopping Game and Zoo Game paradigms to refine task parameters, validate measures, and inform the design of the full EM9 study.
